retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with HELLP syndrome have a 19% to 27% risk of developing HELLP syndrome in subsequent pregnancies. Recurrent cases occur in the latter part of the gestation period and are less severe after two episodes. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pre-eclampsia with DIC: Usually, patients present in the 3rd trimester similar to HELLP syndrome. Pre-eclampsia with DIC Involves clotting factors and causes increased PT and PTT, unlike HELLP syndrome. Patients will have hypertension and proteinuria similar to HELLP syndrome patients.

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: HELLP syndrome has a prevalence of 0.5% to 0.9%. About 70% of cases occur in the third trimester of pregnancy, and the remainder occurs within 48 hours of delivery. [10] The mortality rate of women with HELLP syndrome is 0 to 24%, with a perinatal death rate of up to 37%. [5] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The syndrome of hemolysis with a microangiopathic blood smear, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets in pregnant and postpartum patients is more commonly known by the acronym HELLP syndrome. [1] [2] It may represent a complication or progression of severe preeclampsia; however, this relationship has been challenged by recent evidence that they may be separate disorders as preceding hypertension or proteinuria is absent in at least 15 to 20% of patients with HELLP syndrome. [3] A previous history of preeclampsia or HELLP syndrome can lead to recurrence in subsequent pregnancies. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS): They may have hemolysis and thrombocytopenia. Liver function tests are normal. They may have hypertension and proteinuria like patients with HELLP syndrome. Dominant features of APS are recurrent arterial/venous thrombosis and repeated spontaneous abortions <10 weeks gestation or fetal loss. Elevated Lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin antibodies, and beta2-glycoprotein antibodies are needed for diagnosis. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Intensive care Anesthesiology Pediatrics/neonatology Maternal-fetal medicine Hepatology Surgery Nephrology Hematology Opthalmology

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors for developing HELLP syndrome. Recognize the clinical presentation of a patient with HELLP syndrome and review the diagnostic workup. Outline the management for HELLP syndrome. Emphasize the importance of collaboration amongst different specialties to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes in patients with HELLP syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of HELLP syndrome is unclear but is thought to be a systemic inflammatory disorder mediated by a complement cascade. [5] [7] It is proposed that there may be an overlap with similar pathogenesis as in preeclampsia with poor placentation, but for unknown reasons, it can lead to exaggerated activation of the complement system and greater hepatic inflammation in patients with HELLP syndrome. A subset of HELLP syndrome is caused by complement dysregulation associated with thrombotic microangiopathy and can present with pregnancy-related hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS). [8] Fetal long-chain 3-hydroxy acyl CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (LCHAD) may be contributing to the pathogenesis of HELLP syndrome, but evaluation for these genetic variants is unnecessary as there is no identified role in clinical management. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: HELLP syndrome should be differentiated from other disorders of pregnancy with similar features. [1] [20] The following are some of the important differentials:

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Multiparity and age may contribute to increased risk. [3] [4] [5] Genetic association for increased predisposition for HELLP syndrome has been noted. [3] It has been recently reported that patients with SARS-CoV-2 infection during pregnancy have an increased risk for preeclampsia and HELLP syndrome. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-related hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS) is caused secondary to congenital syndrome leading to complement activation, unlike traditional HUS related to E.coli 0157:H7. They have a TTP-like picture but without fever or AMS. ADAMTS 13 is not lowered. They have increased WVF multimers. Treatment is eculizumab in these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An ischemic-reperfusion injury triggers the systemic inflammatory process in HELLP syndrome. The spiral arteries which fail to remodel because of inadequate trophoblast invasion or defective endothelial apoptosis result in ischemia of the placenta. This causes activation of endothelium, which is accompanied by an increased release of antiangiogenic factors, which leads to multiorgan microvascular injury. In addition, abnormal oxidation of fatty acids by the fetus and release of metabolic intermediates into the mother's circulation causes liver and vascular dysfunction. This occurs when the fetus has an inherited defect in mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation. The inflammatory component includes increased leukocytes and pro-inflammatory cytokines with a reduction in anti-inflammatory cytokines. [1] [7] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients meeting a few of the above criteria are considered to have partial HELLP syndrome and may progress to meet all criteria and hence should be monitored carefully.

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The syndrome of hemolysis with a microangiopathic blood smear, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count, is more commonly known by the acronym HELLP syndrome in pregnant and postpartum patients. It may represent a complication or progression of severe preeclampsia; however, this relationship has been challenged by the recent evidence that these pathologies may have separate etiologies. This activity reviews the etiology, clinical presentation, evaluation, differential diagnosis, and management of HELLP syndrome.

Recognizing these interrelationships supports more precise risk stratification, diagnosis, and management of pregnant patients across the full spectrum of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2600
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with a range of obstetric conditions, including الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (placental inflammation and related sepsis), تسمم الحمل (pregnancy toxemia), تمزق الأغشية الباكر (premature rupture of membranes), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (post‑pregnancy residual findings), and gestation twin (twin gestation). These associations indicate that medical databases contain valuable clinical information on these pregnancy‑related disorders, supporting diagnosis, management, and research across diverse maternal‑fetal health scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 719
retrieved_docs.content: and hemorrhage in pregnancy (نزف في الحمل), underscoring that these specific conditions are particular examples of pregnancy‑related complications. This network of relationships highlights the interconnection of hemorrhagic events with various maternal, placental, and coagulation disorders, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing hemorrhage in pregnancy within comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1636
retrieved_docs.content: abnormalities (Gestation abnormality) are linked as a downstream condition of bleeding in pregnancy, indicating that hemorrhagic events can contribute to broader developmental disturbances of the pregnancy. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how various placental positions, coagulation abnormalities, timing of hemorrhage, and associated hematologic findings converge under the single clinical concept of bleeding in pregnancy, emphasizing the importance of recognizing each subtype for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2073
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompasses several serious obstetric conditions, specifically hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three entities are classified as hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy, representing major sources of maternal morbidity and mortality. Recognizing that hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa fall under the broader category of pregnancy‑related complications highlights their interrelated nature and underscores the importance of prompt diagnosis and coordinated management in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with مقدمات تسمم الحمل (the introductory material on preeclampsia), الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (inflammation of the placenta and membranes, or chorioamnionitis), and تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia). Newborn Screening Terminology includes concepts that are subsets of مقدمات تسمم الحمل and تسمم الحمل, indicating that the terminology used in newborn screening encompasses the introductory aspects of preeclampsia as well as the condition itself. This interconnection underscores the relevance of both databases and newborn screening terminology to the understanding, identification, and clinical management of these obstetric disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium” encompasses a range of adverse conditions that can arise during pregnancy, labor, or the postpartum period, including the specific complication “Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” which is recognized as a distinct type of obstetric complication. Within this broader group, the subcategory “COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY” is positioned as a child of the overall complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that it focuses on adverse events that are primarily associated with the gestational phase itself. Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of obstetric complications, linking general categories to more specific clinical presentations that are important for diagnosis, monitoring, and management in maternal health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: a parent category for hemorrhage in pregnancy, and the higher‑level groups “pregnancy and puerperal disorders” and “maternal pregnancy complications” (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم) are also listed as parents of hemorrhage in pregnancy. This hierarchical structure underscores that hemorrhage in pregnancy is a specific disorder within the larger spectrum of gestational abnormalities and maternal obstetric disorders. Overall, the knowledge base connects hemorrhage in pregnancy with numerous specific bleeding scenarios, coagulation disorders, placental pathologies, and other pregnancy‑related complications, emphasizing the importance of recognizing these interrelated conditions for comprehensive maternal care and effective management during the antenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: hemorrhage and placental bleeding. Collectively, these interrelated entities and relationships illustrate how various uterine and hemorrhagic conditions are organized under the overarching concepts of pregnancy complications, maternal antenatal events, and broader obstetric disorder categories, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing these interconnected obstetric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2892
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for managing diabetes during pregnancy, acute cholestasis of the liver, and fetal distress syndrome, indicating its relevance across these distinct obstetric and hepatic conditions and its role in guiding patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: labor‑complication term “مضاعفات المخاض.” Maternal conditions that complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are organized under “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium.” This includes spotting complicating pregnancy (unspecified trimester) and its early‑trimester manifestation as first‑trimester hemorrhage, as well as hypertension‑related disorders. Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium belongs to the broader class “disease of the circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium,” and is further related to the inverse condition “hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” underscoring hypertension’s central role among pregnancy‑associated circulatory disorders. Hypertensive disorders intersect with other parent categories such as “complications mainly related to pregnancy,” “early or threatened labor,” and “indications for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1910
retrieved_docs.content: Databases serve as important clinical resources that are associated with a range of obstetric conditions. They contain relevant information on الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (chorioamnionitis), a serious infection of the fetal membranes that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes. They also provide data on تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy that poses risks of organ damage and requires careful monitoring. In addition, databases include information on حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (bicornuate pregnancy, a uterine anomaly that can influence fetal development and delivery planning). Finally, they cover قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum), a condition characterized by moderate nausea and vomiting during pregnancy that may require supportive care. By integrating knowledge on these conditions, databases support clinicians in diagnosis, management, and research aimed at improving maternal‑fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1617
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with مقدمات تسمم الحمل and الالتهابُ المشيماءِ والسَّلَى, indicating that these information systems are linked to the clinical contexts of pre‑eclampsia precursors and placental inflammation accompanied by sepsis. This association suggests that data repositories may be utilized to support the diagnosis, monitoring, and research of these obstetric complications, thereby enhancing patient care and informing clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2388
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, including the specific conditions abruptio placenta and placenta previa, are classified together as types of obstetric hemorrhage. This classification groups these distinct pregnancy‑related bleeding disorders, emphasizing their shared relevance to maternal health and guiding clinical assessment and management of hemorrhagic complications in pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.
